blocks.content: He arrived before anyone else. Mr. Alvarez brought coffee without being asked and set it on the table and asked no questions, which was its own kind of generosity.
Ethan sat in the booth with the coffee going warm in his hands and watched the door.

He was not rehearsing anything. He had understood, at some point between last night and this morning, that there was nothing to rehearse. He was not the one saying the difficult thing. He was the one receiving it. And receiving things well — he had thought about this, had been thinking about it since she'd texted: thank you for being the kind of person who thinks about how to receive something rather than just asking for it — receiving things well was not a performance. It was a quality of stillness. Of being present without agenda. Of letting what was being given arrive without trying to shape it into something more manageable.
He would listen. That was all.

He would not interrupt. He would not try to fix it. He would not perform any reaction — not composure, not compassion, not love. He would just be there, the way you are there for something that matters. Fully, quietly, without reservation.

The door opened. Nathan came in first — he always came in first — and then the others behind him, and finally Cassandra, last, in the particular way she sometimes entered rooms: not late, just deliberate. Taking in the space before she entered it.
Their eyes met across the coffee shop.
He gave her a nod. Small. She nodded back.
